items.title: In the world's economic 'black holes,' data still leak out
items.summary: From satellite imagery to clandestine price reports, a new study draws on North Korea to explore economic activity in opaque regimes and information-scarce regions. North Korea is the blackest of economic black holes. Even a basic question like "is the economy shrinking or expanding?" can be difficu

items.title: Why tipping fatigue is growing in Canada
items.summary: Ever feel uncomfortable when a payment screen asks for a tip? We sure have. As tipping prompts become more widespread, more consumers are feeling uneasy or frustrated, but not always sure why.

items.title: Painting the growing season in the Maize Triangle
items.summary: Radar data from an agricultural area in South Africa, shown in a vivid color palette, reveal crop types and how they changed during the Southern Hemisphere's growing season.

items.title: New technology to transform professional development in schools
items.summary: Professional learning and student assessment in schools is set to be transformed, thanks to a first of its kind advance in education technology led by the University of Glasgow.

items.title: NASA Awards Contract for Johnson Space Center Infrastructure
items.summary: NASA has selected seven companies to provide construction, revitalization, and infrastructure improvements at the agency’s Johnson Space Center in Houston. The Johnson Space Center Multiple Award Construction Contract supports up to $300 million in upgrades to mission‑support facilities, utilities,

items.title: NASA Hosts SpaceX Crew-11 Astronauts for Public Event at Headquarters
items.summary: NASA will host a public event featuring three crew members from the agency’s SpaceX Crew-11 mission at 11 a.m. EDT Monday, June 1. The event, which takes place during the crew’s standard postflight visit, will be held in the Webb Auditorium at NASA Headquarters in the Mary W. Jackson building, 300 E

items.title: Modeling the Gulf: A researcher's quest to map every current, particle and tide
items.summary: Understanding the dynamics of how water moves is deceptively simple in concept and endlessly complex in practice. Real-world marine environments are anything but controlled: weather, seasons, and geography change constantly. Yet understanding water movement is a critical aspect in areas of study lik

items.title: Fish-microbe partnership may influence ocean health by making carbon-trapping minerals
items.summary: New research reveals a potential link between the gut microbes of a fish and global ocean processes, offering new insight into how marine ecosystems help regulate ocean chemistry and the marine carbon cycle. The study, titled "Symbiotic bacteria may support calcium carbonate precipitation in the Gul

items.title: Diamond quantum sensor could reveal elusive altermagnets
items.summary: For nearly a century, there were two known kinds of magnets. Ferromagnets are the classic magnets that attract metal and keep pictures stuck to the refrigerator. Antiferromagnets hide their magnetism at the atomic scale but are increasingly prized for their technological potential. A third category

items.title: Predator-triggered orange tails may help tadpoles survive by redirecting deadly bites
items.summary: Bright colors in animals are beautiful but often considered risky because they are more obvious to predators. However, conspicuous colors can also serve defensively, signaling toxicity or even luring predators away from more vulnerable body parts.

items.title: Plants hit the brakes on immunity to survive viral infections
items.summary: When viruses invade a plant, you might expect an all-out immune war. But new research published in Science shows that, much like in humans, too strong an immune response can actually do more harm than good.

items.title: Are taxpayers being gaslighted by street lamp charm?
items.summary: Gas streetlights might look quaint, but researchers at the University of Cincinnati say they are costly, wasteful and release toxic pollutants into the air. In two studies examining their use in Boston, Massachusetts, and Cincinnati, UC researchers found that each lamp releases many times the amount

items.title: Freeze-dried reagents and hand-powered hardware bring biomanufacturing to remote labs
items.summary: Researchers at the University of Toronto's Leslie Dan Faculty of Pharmacy, working with collaborators around the world, have demonstrated the effectiveness of a suite of low-cost, portable biotechnology tools designed to improve access to laboratory research and diagnostics in resource-limited

items.title: Designing catalysts during synthesis could speed cleaner fuels and greener industry
items.summary: The synthesis of materials can serve as a tool for developing smart, adaptive electrocatalysts. This rapidly evolving field of research involves in-situ analytics, data-driven discoveries and autonomous robotics. These new approaches could accelerate the discovery of long-lasting and efficient catal

items.title: Vast botanical data help solve Darwin's puzzle of why some exotic plants become pests
items.summary: There's a conundrum that has perplexed biologists since Charles Darwin himself. Why do some exotic species take off as invasive pests while others don't?

items.title: IceCube detects break in cosmic neutrino spectrum, ruling out simple power-law model
items.summary: A new study published in Physical Review Letters by the IceCube Collaboration reports evidence that the energy spectrum of astrophysical neutrinos is not a simple straight line.

items.title: Citizens as political actors, not individual consumers: New study calls for tighter advertising regulations
items.summary: Commercial marketing oriented toward sustainability is not compatible with degrowth, even when it promotes consuming less. That is the conclusion of a study by ICTA-UAB and the London School of Economics and Political Science.

items.title: Antihydrogen mirrors hydrogen in upgraded spectrum test, narrowing cosmic mystery
items.summary: University of Calgary researchers are a part of a group who just got one step closer to solving a mystery of the universe. Dr. Timothy Friesen, Ph.D., an associate professor of Physics and Astronomy in the Faculty of Science, and his team led a new measurement comparing the spectrum of hydrogen to i

items.title: Elephant declines could trigger wider ecosystem losses in African savannas, 15-year test shows
items.summary: For decades, ecologists have theorized that the extinction of one important species could set off a chain reaction of losses throughout an ecosystem. Now, new research offers some of the clearest real-world evidence that this idea of coextinction is not just theoretical.

items.title: Heat and drought push Europe's trees into survival mode, often fatally
items.summary: The once-majestic oak tree is all but dead: battered by repeated heat waves, it has shut down vital functions to conserve water and is slowly dying in a French forest.
